stand-up-create.title: Stand up a project (create new)
stand-up-create.story: As an operator, I create a brand-new project from Studio's library — the create-new path of the capability diagram. AI-assisted instructions- and project-brain-builders seed its AGENTS.md and its seeded-to-grow knowledge base, while the project builder lets me tune north star, demo timeline, and contract readiness.

su-create-title.title: Title card — Studio library
su-create-title.narration: The library page reports ready before anything else loads, and a title card frames the three things Studio does: author a flow, run it, swap its engine.

checks.msg: library: [data-page="library"][data-page-ready="true"]

captures.caption: Title — "Forge Studio: author a flow, run it, swap its engine"

su-create-library.title: Library — everything is data
su-create-library.narration: The library renders flows, agents, projects, and knowledge bases side by side as data cards, plus an operator-pulse panel — including the very flow the operator will author from scratch later in this walkthrough.

su-create-orientation.title: First-run orientation + discoverable creation
su-create-orientation.narration: With the library already populated, the first-run welcome panel correctly stays hidden, and the "+ New Agent" CTA proves creating something new is one click away, not a URL only a developer would know.

su-create-instructions.title: instructions-creator — generate AGENTS.md (AI-assisted)
su-create-instructions.narration: The operator launches the instructions-creator agent, answers its two clarifying questions, and approves the AGENTS.md it drafts — a new project gets its onboarding contract written for it, with a human still signing off.

su-create-project-brain.narration: The project-brain-builder analyses the new project and stages three seed themes for review; approving commits nothing but a pointer — the project's own knowledge base starts here and grows as cycles run.

su-create-project-builder.title: Project builder — /projects/mdtoc
su-create-project-builder.narration: In the project builder for mdtoc, the operator tunes north star, demo timeline, and contract readiness at a glance; adding a demo step live-flips the dirty flag, proving nothing here is a static page.

agents.title: Compose an agent
agents.story: As an operator, I compose the three OOTB plan/dev/review agents from forge's curated starter library, then reopen an existing agent to prove its composition — skills, tools, runtime, budgets — is editable, not fixed once built.

agents-starters.title: Author plan/dev/review agents from the starter library
agents-starters.narration: The operator picks each of the three curated starters in turn — required fields pre-filled, advanced config collapsed — and saves each straight to a SKILL.md that then passes forge's own `studio lint` gate: the agents pillar's OOTB library, tuned through forge's own development, made concrete.

J2: saving the "dev" starter writes skills/dev/SKILL.md
J2: saving the "review" starter writes skills/review/SKILL.md
J2: `forge studio lint` validates the three authored agents (exit 0)

agents-builder.narration: Reopening the shipped project-manager agent, the operator expands Advanced to see its skill/tool/MCP/hook drop zones and runtime SDK, then edits its purpose field and watches the dirty flag flip before discarding — proof an OOTB agent stays modifiable after the fact.

flows-author.title: Author a flow
flows-author.story: As an operator, I author a brand-new cycle flow entirely as data — the flows pillar's user-creates path — stringing plan/dev/review agents together, handing the result real work, and proving a from-scratch rebuild of forge-develop is structurally identical to the production seed.

flows-author-new-flow.title: String plan/dev/review into a flow (new-flow builder)
flows-author-new-flow.narration: From the library's "+ New Flow" CTA, the canvas seeds itself from the basic starter (plan → dev → review, one verdict gate); the operator names and saves it, drags a node to a new position, and reloads — proving the authored flow, and its hand-arranged layout, both persist and pass `studio lint`.

checks.msg: J3: library "+ New Flow" CTA is enabled and routes to the flow builder
J3: new-flow canvas seeded from the basic starter (≥3 nodes, got 3)

J3: saving the new flow writes studio/flows/my-first-flow/flow.yaml
J3: saved flow persists node positions (every node has numeric x/y; 3 nodes)
J3: authored flow keeps the human verdict gate (zero-gate flows are rejected)
J3: `forge studio lint` validates the authored flow (exit 0)
J3: authored flow is runnable (data-can-start="true", got "true")
J3: dragging node "plan" moved + saved its position (x 120→244)

flows-author-seeded-run.title: Give the authored flow work (seeded run on my-first-flow)
flows-author-seeded-run.narration: The freshly authored flow is handed a real run: its own plan/dev/review hexes progress and park at the verdict gate, with per-phase cost accruing — proof a user-authored flow is a first-class citizen of the monitor, not a demo-only shell.

flows-author-scratch-parity.title: Build the forge-develop flow from scratch (flow-as-data)
flows-author-scratch-parity.narration: Rebuilding forge-develop node-for-node from scratch, `studio lint` validates it and its node set, gate, and edge count match the production seed exactly — the hardcoded cycle really is just one flow definition, and the operator can drag more agents onto it from the same palette.

stand-up-onboard.title: Stand up a project (onboard existing)
stand-up-onboard.story: As an operator, I onboard an existing repository through the UI — the onboard-existing path of the capability diagram — and watch forge deterministically resolve it against the project contract until it is ready for development.

su-onboard-project.title: Onboard a project from the UI
su-onboard-project.narration: Filling in only a name and north star (the quality gate defaults sensibly), the operator onboards a new project from the library; .forge/project.json lands on disk with the hard contract fields, the project appears in the library, and `studio lint` stays green.

su-onboard-preflight.narration: Onboarding a second, existing-style repo trips a real contract clause (no ARTIFACTS convention yet); the operator clicks the one auto-fix action and watches the clause disappear — the repo is aligned to the contract deterministically, no LLM call needed.

skills.title: Compose a skill
skills.story: As an operator, I browse forge's OOTB skill library — highly rated skills sourced from existing community libraries, provenance and stars shown — then edit one in place and author a brand-new skill from scratch.

skills-ootb-library.title: OOTB skill library (community-sourced)
skills-ootb-library.narration: The catalog ships a library of community-sourced skills (superpowers, TDD, security-review, and more), each one carrying its source URL, provenance, and star count in the agent builder's palette — the operator can see exactly where a skill came from before dragging it in.

checks.msg: SK-1: catalog ships an OOTB skill library (9 community-skills)

captures.caption: Part 2 (skills) — the OOTB skill library, sourced from community repos

skills-edit.title: Edit a skill
skills-edit.narration: Opening an OOTB skill in the builder, the operator rewrites its instructions body and saves — the change lands straight in that skill's own SKILL.md, proving an out-of-the-box skill is editable text, not a black box.

checks.msg: SK-2: editor loaded the skill ([data-agent-id="journey-ootb-review"])

skills-create.title: Author a new skill
skills-create.narration: From a blank builder the operator names a brand-new skill, describes it in one line, and writes its instructions; creating it writes a fresh SKILL.md to the library — the skills pillar's third leg, adding new skills from scratch.

flows-run.title: Run a gated cycle
flows-run.story: As an operator, I run a gated cycle end-to-end on a real mdtoc feature — idea to architect interview to PLAN gate to an autonomous build to a review gate to merge to reflection — monitoring flow progress and clearing every gate myself from the flow UI.

flows-run-idea.title: Operator drops the mdtoc idea
flows-run-idea.narration: The operator types one real mdtoc feature idea into a single field and hits go — no form, no ceremony — and forge opens a fresh architect interview session to run with it.

checks.msg: [data-section="new-idea"] present on /architect/new
[data-action="start-architect"] navigates to /architect/<sid>/interview

captures.caption: R1 — operator types a real mdtoc feature idea

flows-run-grounding.title: Architect grounds itself — P3 activity panel
flows-run-grounding.narration: Before asking a single question, the architect reads the CLI source and the brain — every tool call and reasoning line streams live into the activity panel, so the operator watches it ground itself in the real codebase rather than guess.

checks.msg: P3: [data-section="architect-activity"] rendered
P3: activity panel data-activity-count ≥1 (got 8)
P3: at least one reasoning row rendered in the activity panel

captures.caption: R1 (mid-stream) — P3 activity panel filling while the architect reads the CLI source
R1 (settled) — P3 activity panel: tool calls + reasoning rows persisted

flows-run-questions.title: Architect returns questions
flows-run-questions.narration: The architect comes back with exactly two clarifying questions — schema default and acceptance fixture — asking only what it genuinely cannot resolve on its own.

checks.msg: [data-section="architect-interview"] rendered with questions
architect returned ≥2 questions (found 2, want ≥2)

captures.caption: R1 — architect returns 2 clarifying questions (schema design + acc fixture)

flows-run-freetext.title: Operator answers — P2 free-text override on Q2
flows-run-freetext.narration: The operator answers Q1 with a radio option but overrides Q2 entirely in free text; the question resolves on the typed answer and every radio stays unselected — proving the operator is never boxed into the offered choices.

checks.msg: P2: Q2 [data-question-resolved="true"] after free-text entry
P2: all Q2 radio options unselected — free-text overrides the radio

captures.caption: R1 — P2: operator types a free-text answer on Q2 (overriding the option list)
R1 — planning: architect drafts with the answers folded in

flows-run-stall.title: Stall cameo — P1 StuckWarning
flows-run-stall.narration: A staged stale heartbeat makes the architect look stuck for two minutes and the StuckWarning lights up; once the session resumes the warning clears on its own — the operator always sees when forge has gone quiet, and when it hasn't.

checks.msg: P1: [data-architect-stale="true"] rendered when session staleMs > 120s
P1: [data-architect-stale] clears after session refresh

captures.caption: R1 — P1: StuckWarning renders when the architect goes quiet for >2 min

flows-run-draft-cost.title: Architect drafts — P4 real cost
flows-run-draft-cost.narration: The architect emits its plan and the hex greens at $0.00 — that's not a bug: real cycles meter the architect turn out-of-cycle, its duration tracked but its dollar cost billed elsewhere, and the demo owns that honestly rather than hiding it.

captures.caption: R1 — P4: architect hex greens ($0.00 — real cycles meter it out-of-cycle)

flows-run-plan-gate.title: Rich PLAN.html (gate)
flows-run-plan-gate.narration: The plan gate presents the architect's output as rendered Given/When/Then acceptance-criteria cards, not raw markdown — the same PLAN.html the PM will read verbatim once approved.

checks.msg: plan gate renders the rich PLAN.html iframe

captures.caption: R2 — rich PLAN.html with Given/When/Then AC cards

flows-run-send-back.title: Send-back + revised plan
flows-run-send-back.narration: The operator sends the plan back with one concrete piece of feedback (cover the no-markers case); the architect reruns and re-presents a revised plan carrying a "(revised)" badge — human gate #1, working as a real gate.

captures.caption: R2 — operator sends the plan back with feedback
R2 — revised plan re-presented with (revised) badge

flows-run-approve.title: Approve → watch it build
flows-run-approve.narration: Approving the plan hands off to the second flow, Forge Develop; clicking "Watch it build" lands on its monitor, which shows only the develop slice's own hexes — while the same threaded run's architect slice, checked separately, sits complete on its own flow at that honest $0.00.

checks.msg: monitor: forge-develop slice shows its phase hexes (unifier/review) (found 3, want ≥2)
P4: architect hex (on /flows/forge-architect) reaches complete (cost is $0 — metered out-of-cycle)
monitor: forge-architect slice shows the pm hex (architect+pm, not the develop nodes)

captures.caption: R2 — operator approves the plan (human decision #1 complete)
R2 — "Watch it build →" lands on the Studio flow monitor
R2 — Forge Develop monitor shows the build slice live (run rail + topology)

flows-run-pm-decompose.title: PM decomposes ACs into work items
flows-run-pm-decompose.narration: The project-manager phase turns the plan's acceptance criteria into two dependency-ordered work items straight from Given/When/Then, not vague tasks; clicking either the phase hex or a WI hex opens its own drawer of detail.

checks.msg: monitor: PM materialised ≥2 WI hexes (found 2, want ≥2)
monitor phase drawer: clicking a phase hex opens the drawer (data-hex-kind="phase")
monitor WI drawer: clicking a wi hex opens the drawer (data-hex-kind="wi")

flows-run-tdd-red.title: Dev-loop TDD red — gate.expected-fail
flows-run-tdd-red.narration: The dev-loop's first move on WI-1 is a failing test — gate.expected-fail fires before any implementation exists — TDD is the loop's actual discipline, not a claim in a prompt.

captures.caption: R3 — TDD red: gate.expected-fail — the inject test fails before src/inject.ts exists

flows-run-grind.title: Dev-loop GRIND (fast-forward)
flows-run-grind.narration: The dev-loop keeps grinding on WI-1 (fast-forwarded here): tool calls accumulate and the token/cost bar climbs live — the operator can watch the actual work happen, not a spinner.

captures.caption: R3 (fast-forward) — dev-loop implementing WI-1; token/cost bar growing

flows-run-dependency-gate.title: Gate.pass + WI-1 green → WI-2 starts
flows-run-dependency-gate.narration: WI-1's gate passes and only then does WI-2 — which declared WI-1 as a dependency — begin: the scheduler enforces the ordering the plan declared, visibly, rather than racing both work items at once.

captures.caption: R3 — gate.pass; WI-1 green; WI-2 (depends on WI-1) only now starts
R3 — dev-loop hex greens (both WIs done); unifier runs next on its own hex

flows-run-unifier.title: Unifier on its own hex
flows-run-unifier.narration: With both work items green, the unifier phase's own hex activates and merges them into one branch, runs the gate, and authors the demo — the seam between many parallel WIs and one reviewable change, made visible.

checks.msg: monitor: unifier node lit its own status complete (not folded into dev-loop)

captures.caption: R3 (mid-pulse) — unifier hex active, running the gate + acceptance on the merged branch
R3 — unifier (own node) greens after authoring the demo

flows-run-cost-rollup.title: Cost rollup
flows-run-cost-rollup.narration: The cycle badge sums exactly what dev-loop and unifier already accrued ($1.75 + $1.20) — the rollup is arithmetic on real per-phase numbers the operator watched tick up, not a separate estimate.

checks.msg: monitor: cost rollup — a phase hex shows cost > 0
monitor: run rail shows the cycle as gated (ready-for-review)

captures.caption: R3 — cost rollup across the spine (Studio monitor)

flows-run-review-comment.narration: The review page renders the actual DEMO.md with a before/after slider and per-region comment anchors; AC-2 reads PARTIAL — a real gap surfaces before the operator ever has to decide anything.

flows-run-review-send-back.title: Send-back — operator anchors a blocking comment to AC-2
flows-run-review-send-back.narration: The operator anchors a blocking comment directly on AC-2 instead of filling out a form; the page derives a send-back verdict from that comment alone, and it survives a reload — progressing the gate through the flow UI, on the artifact itself.

flows-run-rerun.title: Dev-loop reruns on feedback (fast-forward)
flows-run-rerun.narration: The anchored comment routes straight back to the dev-loop, which reruns on exactly that criterion and re-renders the demo — the send-back was real routing, not a dead-end button.

captures.caption: R4 (fast-forward) — dev-loop reran on the new criterion; back to "Review →"

flows-run-re-review.title: Re-review — PARTIAL→MET
flows-run-re-review.narration: AC-2 now reads MET; resolving the original blocking comment flips the page's derived verdict from send-back back to approve — the loop closed on the operator's own criterion, not a generic re-run.

checks.msg: re-review: partial AC count == 0 after dev-loop rerun (got 0)
re-review: all ACs show verdict "met" (found 2, want ≥2)
resolving the blocking comment flips the derived verdict to approve

captures.caption: R4 — re-review: AC-2 now MET (PARTIAL→MET payoff)

flows-run-approve-merge.title: Approve & merge → completed spine
flows-run-approve-merge.narration: The operator approves and the PR merges for real; the run rail deliberately holds at "active" rather than "complete" while reflection is still in flight — a merged cycle genuinely isn't done until its lesson lands, and the monitor tells the truth about that.

flows-run-reflect.title: Reflect
flows-run-reflect.narration: The operator answers the reflection questions — WI sizing, repeated actions, a free-text lesson — and only once that submits does the reflector write a brain theme and the run rail finally flips to complete.

checks.msg: reflection node greened after tuning feedback (/flows/forge-reflect slice)

flows-run-monitor-deep-dive.title: Flow monitor deep-dive — /flows/forge-develop (Model B develop slice)
flows-run-monitor-deep-dive.narration: On a freshly gated run, clicking the unifier hex opens its own drawer of gate sub-checks and phase log — every phase and WI hex from a live cycle stays this inspectable, not just while it's running.

flows-run-start-run-cta.narration: On the from-scratch forge-develop-scratch flow — never yet run — the Start Run button is live and enabled, proving the engine can launch any authored flow directly from the UI, not only the seeded production ones.

flows-run-gate-control.title: Engine — gate control + cost on the gated run
flows-run-gate-control.narration: A second run parks itself at its gate and links straight through to the verdict screen, its accrued cost metered against the flow's own ceiling — the operator progresses this gate from the monitor, not a separate tool.

roadmap.title: Project roadmap
roadmap.story: As an operator, I browse a project's serpentine roadmap of initiatives over time and click "Start development" on a pending one — the roadmap extension proving the initiative timeline actually drives what forge builds next, not just a passive history view.

roadmap-tab.title: Per-project Roadmap tab
roadmap-tab.narration: The Roadmap tab renders every initiative as a dot on a serpentine timeline; clicking a completed one pops a detail card listing its actual work items — the roadmap is read straight off real cycle history, not a hand-maintained list.

checks.msg: roadmap: [data-section="project-roadmap"] rendered
roadmap: ≥1 [data-roadmap-node] on the timeline (got 2)
roadmap: clicking a dot pops its card with ≥1 [data-work-item-id] (got 1)

captures.caption: R6 — per-project Roadmap tab: the serpentine timeline of initiatives over time
R6 — clicking an initiative dot pops its detail card up off the timeline

roadmap-start-development.title: Start development trigger (DEC-3)
roadmap-start-development.narration: A decomposed-but-not-yet-built initiative offers "Start development" right on its roadmap card; clicking it repoints the manifest at the forge-develop flow and threads the architect-minted cycle id — the timeline itself is the trigger, not a separate queue command.

checks.msg: roadmap: the decomposed initiative is pending (develop-able)

swap-runtime.title: Swap the runtime adapter
swap-runtime.story: As an operator, I open an agent's runtime picker to prove the SDK/model choice is a registry-driven seam — claude selectable today, gemini and codex visibly disabled until their adapters ship, with a range strategy for routing across model tiers.

swap-runtime-sdk-picker.title: Runtime-adapter seam — /agents/project-manager
swap-runtime-sdk-picker.narration: On the project-manager agent, claude's SDK card is selectable while codex and gemini sit visibly disabled (no adapter registered yet); switching to the range strategy picks multiple Claude tiers and the YAML preview updates live to `strategy: range` — the swap seam, not a config file the operator has to hand-edit.

knowledge.title: Knowledge graph
knowledge.story: As an operator, I browse the real cycles brain as a force-graph, pin a piece of human guidance onto it, and run lint/index maintenance — the knowledge pillar's OOTB brains, edited through both deterministic tooling and my own guidance-plus-ingest loop.

knowledge-graph.title: KB-backend seam — /knowledge?id=cycles (real brain)
knowledge-graph.narration: The knowledge screen force-graphs the real cycles brain — theme and index nodes, KB health panel, a backend selector — and clicking a theme node opens its full article; this is the actual OOTB cross-cycle brain, not a mock graph.

knowledge-pin-guidance.title: KB-backend seam — pin-guidance
knowledge-pin-guidance.narration: The operator types a lesson straight into the HUMAN GUIDANCE panel and pins it; a guidance node appears in the graph immediately — human guidance is how the brain grows between ingest passes, visible as its own node until the next one folds it in.

knowledge-lint-index.title: KB maintenance — lint / index / OOTB brains
knowledge-lint-index.narration: The operator runs the real kb-lint and kb-index actions from the maintenance panel — structural checks and a regenerated index, not cosmetic buttons — and the KB selector confirms both cycles and forge-dev ship as OOTB brains: the knowledge pillar is edited through lint/index tooling, on top of the human-guidance loop from the beat before.

recovery.title: Recover a stuck initiative
recovery.story: As an operator, I open the dedicated recovery screen to inspect, requeue, or abandon a stuck cycle — the recovery extension, proving forge surfaces its own failure states in the UI rather than requiring me to dig through logs or a CLI.

recovery-surface.title: Recovery surface — the operator surface for stuck cycles (DEC-6)
recovery-surface.narration: The /recovery screen renders every stuck initiative (or a clean empty state when there are none) with inspect/requeue/abandon actions — the CLI verbs that used to handle this moved here, so a stuck cycle is recovered in-UI, not by an operator dropping into a terminal.

checks.msg: recovery: [data-page="recovery"][data-page-ready="true"] renders (DEC-6 operator surface)
recovery: the recoverable-list or empty-state section renders

captures.caption: S4 — Recovery: inspect/requeue/abandon a stuck cycle, all in the UI (CLI retired)

demo-builder.title: Regenerate the demo page
demo-builder.story: As the operator, I regenerate a project's demo page element by element — brief the agent, watch it compose the capture/verify/present trio, review the result, and lock it in as the reproducible artifact.

demo-builder-brief.title: Brief the demo agent
demo-builder-brief.narration: Before the agent touches anything, the operator types one line of steering ("give the CLI capture more contrast") into a real briefing field — the regenerate isn't a blind rerun, it takes direction first.

demo-builder-generate.title: The demo agent composes the page, element by element
demo-builder-generate.narration: The agent works through all three demo-process steps — capture, verify, present — each one bound to its own element, then hands back an iframe preview for the operator to review before anything is locked in.

demo-builder-lock.title: Lock the demo in
demo-builder-lock.narration: Locking writes demo.lock.json plus a history entry to disk and returns the operator to the project — the regenerated demo becomes the one reproducible artifact for this cycle, not a throwaway preview.
